narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid family background in the high income bracket, you've put significant effort into your law school journey, including scoring a strong 38 on your LSAT and committing to full-time studies, alongside your undergraduate GPA of 2.90. Unfortunately, the university's admission model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 41.6% chance that you would fail the bar exam—high enough to lead to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass on their first try. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is notably lower than the typical student's around the middle of the pack, signaling to the model a potential struggle in later academic performance. This outweighed your impressive LSAT score, which was above the average of about 36.7 and strongly pulled the prediction toward expecting you to pass. Your high family income also worked in your favor, nudging the model toward success compared to most students from lower brackets. Your Year 1 decile of 1 had a smaller but still negative pull toward failure, while your undergraduate GPA, though below the average 3.21, had minimal impact. Being full-time, like over 90% of students, barely factored in. Overall, your profile stands out for strong test scores and background but is held back by those early and later low decile rankings relative to peers who typically rank higher and pass the bar. While this is disappointing, it's not the end of the road—consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school deciles, perhaps supplementing with targeted bar prep to demonstrate growth. You could also apply to schools with more holistic reviews or reapply after strengthening these areas, as many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing weak spots head-on. We're rooting for you to turn this around.
